title: Mastering Linear Equations
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
A linear equation is an equation where the variable has an exponent of 1. The standard form is ax + b = c, where x is the variable, and a, b, and c are constants.

To solve a linear equation, the goal is to isolate the variable on one side of the equals sign. Whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other — this keeps the equation balanced.

Step-by-step method:
1. Simplify both sides of the equation separately (combine like terms, remove parentheses)
2. Move all terms with the variable to one side (use addition or subtraction)
3. Move all constant terms to the other side
4. Divide both sides by the coefficient of the variable
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

flashcards.title: Key Point 1
flashcards.body: A linear equation is an equation where the variable has an exponent of 1. The standard form is ax + b = c, where x is the variable, and a, b, and c are constants.

flashcards.title: Key Point 2
flashcards.body: To solve a linear equation, the goal is to isolate the variable on one side of the equals sign. Whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other — this keeps the equation balanced.

title: Practice Questions: Linear Equations (Part 2)
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
Graphing linear equations helps visualize the relationship between two variables. The standard form is y = mx + b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept.

The slope (m) measures how steep the line is. It is calculated as rise over run: change in y divided by change in x. A positive slope goes up as x increases, a negative slope goes down.

The y-intercept (b) is where the line crosses the y-axis (when x = 0). To graph a line: plot the y-intercept, then use the slope to find another point, and draw the line through both points.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: To identify areas that need more practice

flashcards.title: Key Point 1
flashcards.body: Graphing linear equations helps visualize the relationship between two variables. The standard form is y = mx + b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept.

title: Hands-On: Linear Equations (Part 3)
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
Systems of linear equations involve two or more equations with the same variables. The solution is the point where the lines intersect.

Three methods to solve:
1. Graphing: Graph both lines and find the intersection point
2. Substitution: Solve one equation for a variable, then substitute into the other
3. Elimination: Add or subtract equations to eliminate a variable

Example: Solve y = 2x + 1 and y = -x + 4 using substitution.
2x + 1 = -x + 4 → 3x = 3 → x = 1, y = 3. Intersection point: (1, 3).
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Check your work against the solution

flashcards.title: Key Point 1
flashcards.body: Systems of linear equations involve two or more equations with the same variables. The solution is the point where the lines intersect.

title: At a Glance: Linear Equations (Part 4)
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
Slope-intercept form (y = mx + b) makes it easy to understand a line's behavior at a glance. The slope tells you the direction and steepness, while the intercept tells you the starting point.

Parallel lines have the same slope but different y-intercepts. Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other (m₁ × m₂ = -1).

Example: Line A: y = 2x + 3, Line B: y = 2x - 5. These are parallel (same slope 2). Line C: y = -½x + 1 is perpendicular to both.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: The main ideas and how they connect

flashcards.body: Slope-intercept form (y = mx + b) makes it easy to understand a line's behavior at a glance. The slope tells you the direction and steepness, while the intercept tells you the starting point.

title: A Complete Guide to Linear Equations (Part 5)
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
Standard form Ax + By = C is another way to write linear equations. To convert to slope-intercept form, solve for y: y = -(A/B)x + (C/B).

The x-intercept is found by setting y = 0 and solving for x. The y-intercept is found by setting x = 0 and solving for y.

Example: 3x + 2y = 6. x-intercept: 3x = 6, x = 2, point (2, 0). y-intercept: 2y = 6, y = 3, point (0, 3).
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

flashcards.body: Standard form Ax + By = C is another way to write linear equations. To convert to slope-intercept form, solve for y: y = -(A/B)x + (C/B).

flashcards.title: Key Point 2
flashcards.body: The x-intercept is found by setting y = 0 and solving for x. The y-intercept is found by setting x = 0 and solving for y.

title: Mastering Linear Equations (Part 6)
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
Linear inequalities are like linear equations but with <, >, ≤, or ≥ instead of =. The solution is a region of the coordinate plane rather than a single line.

To graph: Draw the boundary line (solid for ≤ or ≥, dashed for < or >). Then shade the region that satisfies the inequality. Test a point (like 0, 0) to determine which side to shade.

Example: y > 2x - 3. Draw dashed line y = 2x - 3. Test (0, 0): 0 > -3? Yes. Shade above the line.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: By breaking problems into smaller steps

flashcards.title: Key Point 2

title: Test Your Knowledge: Linear Equations (Part 7)
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
The distance between two points (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) is found using d = √[(x₂ - x₁)² + (y₂ - y₁)²]. This is derived from the Pythagorean theorem.

The midpoint between two points is ((x₁ + x₂)/2, (y₁ + y₂)/2).

These formulas are useful in geometry, navigation, and physics for calculating distances and center points.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Take a break and come back later

title: Introduction to Linear Equations (Part 8)
description: Learn about linear equations in Algebra.
body:
Word problems with linear equations require translating English sentences into algebraic expressions. Look for keywords: 'sum' means +, 'difference' means -, 'product' means ×, 'is' or 'equals' means =.

Strategy: Define your variable, write an equation, solve it, and check your answer against the original problem.

Example: 'The sum of a number and 12 is 35.' → x + 12 = 35 → x = 23.
subject: Algebra

flashcards.title: Key Point 2
flashcards.body: Strategy: Define your variable, write an equation, solve it, and check your answer against the original problem.

title: Deep Dive into Polynomials
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
A polynomial is an expression with variables and coefficients combined using addition, subtraction, and multiplication. Each term has a variable raised to a non-negative integer exponent.

Examples: 3x² + 2x - 5 (quadratic trinomial), 4x⁵ - 2x³ + x - 7 (degree 5 polynomial).

The degree of a polynomial is the highest exponent. The leading coefficient is the coefficient of the term with the highest degree.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

flashcards.title: Key Point 1
flashcards.body: A polynomial is an expression with variables and coefficients combined using addition, subtraction, and multiplication. Each term has a variable raised to a non-negative integer exponent.

flashcards.title: Key Point 2
flashcards.body: Examples: 3x² + 2x - 5 (quadratic trinomial), 4x⁵ - 2x³ + x - 7 (degree 5 polynomial).

title: Practice Questions: Polynomials (Part 2)
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
Adding and subtracting polynomials involves combining like terms — terms with the same variable and exponent. Only the coefficients change; the variables and exponents stay the same.

Example: (3x² + 2x - 5) + (x² - 4x + 1) = 4x² - 2x - 4.

Subtraction: (3x² + 2x - 5) - (x² - 4x + 1) = 2x² + 6x - 6. Remember to distribute the minus sign!
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: To identify areas that need more practice

flashcards.title: Key Point 1
flashcards.body: Adding and subtracting polynomials involves combining like terms — terms with the same variable and exponent. Only the coefficients change; the variables and exponents stay the same.

title: Exercise: Polynomials (Part 3)
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
Multiplying polynomials uses the distributive property. Multiply each term in the first polynomial by each term in the second, then combine like terms.

FOIL method for binomials: First, Outer, Inner, Last.
Example: (x + 3)(x + 5) = x² + 5x + 3x + 15 = x² + 8x + 15.

For larger polynomials, use the box method or distribute systematically.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Check your work against the solution

flashcards.title: Key Point 1
flashcards.body: Multiplying polynomials uses the distributive property. Multiply each term in the first polynomial by each term in the second, then combine like terms.

title: Visual Summary: Polynomials (Part 4)
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
Factoring polynomials reverses multiplication. It means writing a polynomial as a product of simpler polynomials.

Greatest Common Factor (GCF): Factor out the largest term that divides all terms.
Example: 6x³ + 9x² = 3x²(2x + 3).

Difference of squares: a² - b² = (a + b)(a - b). Example: x² - 25 = (x + 5)(x - 5).
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: The main ideas and how they connect

flashcards.body: Factoring polynomials reverses multiplication. It means writing a polynomial as a product of simpler polynomials.

flashcards.title: Key Point 2

title: Understanding Polynomials (Part 5)
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
Factoring trinomials of the form x² + bx + c: Find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b.

Example: x² + 7x + 12. Numbers that multiply to 12: (1, 12), (2, 6), (3, 4). Which add to 7? 3 + 4 = 7. So x² + 7x + 12 = (x + 3)(x + 4).

For ax² + bx + c where a ≠ 1, use the grouping method: Find two numbers that multiply to ac and add to b, then group and factor.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Mastering Polynomials (Part 6)
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
Special polynomial products are patterns that appear frequently:

(a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
(a - b)² = a² - 2ab + b²
(a + b)(a - b) = a² - b²

Examples: (x + 4)² = x² + 8x + 16. (3x - 2)² = 9x² - 12x + 4.

Recognizing these patterns saves time when factoring and expanding.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: By breaking problems into smaller steps

title: Quick Quiz: Polynomials (Part 7)
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
Long division of polynomials works similar to numeric division. Divide the leading term of the dividend by the leading term of the divisor, multiply, subtract, and bring down the next term.

Synthetic division is a shortcut when dividing by (x - c). It uses only the coefficients.

The Remainder Theorem: If polynomial P(x) is divided by (x - c), the remainder equals P(c).

The Factor Theorem: If P(c) = 0, then (x - c) is a factor of P(x).
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Take a break and come back later

title: A Complete Guide to Polynomials (Part 8)
description: Learn about polynomials in Algebra.
body:
Applications of polynomials include modeling real-world phenomena. The height of a projectile follows a quadratic polynomial h(t) = -16t² + v₀t + h₀.

Volume of a box: V = lwh. If dimensions are expressed as polynomials, the volume is a polynomial.

Area calculations often involve polynomial expressions, especially when working with shapes that have variable dimensions.
subject: Algebra

title: Exploring Quadratic Equations
description: Learn about quadratic equations in Algebra.
body:
A quadratic equation has the form ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0. The graph of a quadratic function y = ax² + bx + c is a parabola.

Standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0. Vertex form: a(x - h)² + k = 0, where (h, k) is the vertex.

The vertex is the highest or lowest point on the parabola. If a > 0, the parabola opens up (minimum). If a < 0, it opens down (maximum).
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Check Your Understanding: Quadratic Equations (Part 2)
description: Learn about quadratic equations in Algebra.
body:
Solving quadratics by factoring: Write the equation as ax² + bx + c = 0, factor the left side, set each factor equal to zero, and solve.

Example: x² + 5x + 6 = 0. Factor: (x + 2)(x + 3) = 0. Solutions: x = -2 and x = -3.

Always check your solutions by plugging them back into the original equation.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: To identify areas that need more practice

flashcards.body: Solving quadratics by factoring: Write the equation as ax² + bx + c = 0, factor the left side, set each factor equal to zero, and solve.

title: Step by Step: Quadratic Equations (Part 3)
description: Learn about quadratic equations in Algebra.
body:
The quadratic formula solves any quadratic equation: x = [-b ± √(b² - 4ac)] / (2a).

The discriminant (b² - 4ac) determines the number and type of solutions:
• b² - 4ac > 0: Two distinct real solutions
• b² - 4ac = 0: One real solution (double root)
• b² - 4ac < 0: Two complex solutions
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Check your work against the solution

flashcards.body: The quadratic formula solves any quadratic equation: x = [-b ± √(b² - 4ac)] / (2a).

title: Overview: Quadratic Equations (Part 4)
description: Learn about quadratic equations in Algebra.
body:
Completing the square transforms ax² + bx + c = 0 into a(x - h)² + k = 0. This reveals the vertex and axis of symmetry.

Steps:
1. Move c to the right side: x² + bx = -c
2. Add (b/2)² to both sides
3. Factor the left as (x + b/2)²
4. Take square root of both sides
5. Solve for x

Example: x² + 6x + 5 = 0 → x² + 6x = -5 → x² + 6x + 9 = 4 → (x + 3)² = 4 → x = -3 ± 2 → x = -1 or x = -5.
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: The main ideas and how they connect

flashcards.body: Move c to the right side: x² + bx = -c

title: Mastering Quadratic Equations (Part 5)
description: Learn about quadratic equations in Algebra.
body:
The axis of symmetry of a parabola is the vertical line x = -b/(2a). This passes through the vertex.

The vertex's x-coordinate is -b/(2a). The y-coordinate is found by plugging this back into the equation.

Example: y = 2x² + 8x + 3. Axis: x = -8/(4) = -2. Vertex y: 2(4) + 8(-2) + 3 = 8 - 16 + 3 = -5. Vertex: (-2, -5).
subject: Algebra
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Exploring Quadratic Equations (Part 6)
description: Learn about quadratic equations in Algebra.
body:
Graphing quadratics: Plot the vertex, axis of symmetry, y-intercept (c), and x-intercepts (roots). The parabola opens up if a > 0, down if a < 0.

The y-intercept is always (0, c). The x-intercepts are the solutions to ax² + bx + c = 0.

For a quick sketch: vertex, y-intercept, and one point on each side of the axis.
subject: Algebra

title: Check Your Understanding: Quadratic Equations (Part 7)
description: Learn about quadratic equations in Algebra.
body:
Applications of quadratics include projectile motion (h = -16t² + v₀t + h₀), area optimization (maximizing rectangular area with fixed perimeter), and revenue/cost analysis.

The maximum or minimum value is always at the vertex of the parabola.

Example: A rectangle has perimeter 100. Maximize area. Width = w, length = 50 - w. Area = w(50 - w) = -w² + 50w. Vertex at w = -50/(2(-1)) = 25. Max area = 25 × 25 = 625.
subject: Algebra

title: Mastering Cell Biology
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
The cell is the basic unit of life. All living organisms are made of cells — some have just one (unicellular), others have trillions (multicellular).

Two main cell types:
1. Prokaryotic cells: Simple, no nucleus. Bacteria are prokaryotes. DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm.
2. Eukaryotic cells: Complex, have a nucleus that holds DNA. Plants, animals, fungi, and protists are eukaryotes.

Key organelles in animal cells:
• Nucleus: The control center. Contains DNA and directs cell activities.
• Mitochondria: The power plant. Converts glucose into ATP (energy).
• Ribosomes: Protein factories.
• Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER): Transportation network.
• Golgi Apparatus: The shipping center.
• Cell Membrane: The gatekeeper.
subject: Biology
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Quick Quiz: Cell Biology (Part 2)
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
The cell membrane is a phospholipid bilayer that controls what enters and exits the cell. It is selectively permeable — some substances pass through freely, while others need help.

The fluid mosaic model describes the membrane as a flexible structure with proteins floating in a sea of phospholipids. Proteins serve as channels, pumps, and receptors.

Passive transport requires no energy: diffusion, osmosis, and facilitated diffusion.
Active transport requires energy (ATP): protein pumps, endocytosis, and exocytosis.
subject: Biology

title: Practice: Cell Biology (Part 3)
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
Mitochondria are often called the power plant of the cell. They convert glucose into ATP (adenosine triphosphate) through cellular respiration.

The process has three stages:
1. Glycolysis (in cytoplasm): Breaks glucose into pyruvate, produces 2 ATP
2. Krebs Cycle (in mitochondrial matrix): Produces electron carriers
3. Electron Transport Chain (inner mitochondrial membrane): Produces ~34 ATP

Total: ~36-38 ATP per glucose molecule.
subject: Biology
quizOptions: Check your work against the solution

flashcards.body: Mitochondria are often called the power plant of the cell. They convert glucose into ATP (adenosine triphosphate) through cellular respiration.

title: Overview: Cell Biology (Part 4)
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
Photosynthesis occurs in plant cells' chloroplasts. It converts sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water into glucose and oxygen.

The equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + sunlight → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

Two stages:
1. Light-dependent reactions: Capture sunlight, produce ATP and NADPH, release O₂
2. Calvin Cycle (light-independent): Uses ATP and NADPH to fix CO₂ into glucose
subject: Biology

title: Exploring Cell Biology (Part 5)
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
Cell division is essential for growth, repair, and reproduction. The cell cycle has phases: G₁, S, G₂, and M (mitosis).

Mitosis (nuclear division) has 4 phases:
1. Prophase: Chromosomes condense, nuclear envelope breaks down
2. Metaphase: Chromosomes line up at the center
3. Anaphase: Sister chromatids separate and move to opposite ends
4. Telophase: Two new nuclei form

Cytokinesis follows, dividing the cytoplasm.
subject: Biology

title: A Complete Guide to Cell Biology (Part 6)
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
Meiosis produces sex cells (gametes) with half the chromosome number. It involves two rounds of division: Meiosis I and Meiosis II.

Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes. Meiosis II separates sister chromatids.

The result is 4 genetically unique haploid cells. This genetic variation comes from crossing over (exchange of DNA segments between homologous chromosomes) and independent assortment.
subject: Biology

title: Check Your Understanding: Cell Biology (Part 7)
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
Cell communication is how cells coordinate activities. Signal molecules (ligands) bind to receptors on the target cell's surface or inside the cell.

Types of signaling:
• Autocrine: Cell signals itself
• Paracrine: Cell signals nearby cells
• Endocrine: Cell signals distant cells via hormones
• Synaptic: Neurons transmit signals across synapses

Signal transduction pathways amplify and process signals inside the cell.
subject: Biology

title: A Complete Guide to Cell Biology (Part 8)
description: Learn about cell biology in Biology.
body:
Stem cells are undifferentiated cells that can develop into different cell types. They have two properties: self-renewal (making more stem cells) and differentiation (becoming specialized cells).

Types:
• Embryonic stem cells: Pluripotent — can become any cell type
• Adult stem cells: Multipotent — limited to certain cell types
• Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs): Adult cells reprogrammed to act like embryonic stem cells

Stem cell research offers potential treatments for spinal cord injury, Parkinson's, diabetes, and more.
subject: Biology

title: A Complete Guide to Genetics
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
Genetics is the study of heredity — how traits pass from parents to offspring. The fundamental unit of heredity is the gene, a segment of DNA that codes for a specific protein.

Gregor Mendel (1822-1884) discovered the basic principles of genetics by experimenting with pea plants. He tracked seven traits across generations.

Mendel's Laws:
1. Law of Segregation: Each organism has two alleles for each trait, which separate during gamete formation
2. Law of Independent Assortment: Genes for different traits separate independently

title: Check Your Understanding: Genetics (Part 2)
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a double helix molecule that carries genetic instructions. It is made of nucleotides, each containing a sugar, phosphate, and nitrogen base.

The four bases: Adenine (A), Thymine (T), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C).
Base pairing: A pairs with T, G pairs with C.

Genes are segments of DNA that code for proteins. The human genome has about 20,000-25,000 genes.

flashcards.body: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a double helix molecule that carries genetic instructions. It is made of nucleotides, each containing a sugar, phosphate, and nitrogen base.

title: Step by Step: Genetics (Part 3)
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
DNA replication is the process of copying DNA before cell division. It is semi-conservative — each new DNA molecule has one original strand and one new strand.

Key enzymes:
• Helicase: Unwinds the double helix
• DNA polymerase: Adds complementary bases
• Ligase: Seals gaps between fragments

The replication fork is the Y-shaped region where DNA is actively being copied.
subject: Biology
quizOptions: Check your work against the solution

title: Overview: Genetics (Part 4)
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
Mutations are changes in DNA sequence. They can be:
• Point mutations: Single base change (substitution, insertion, deletion)
• Frameshift mutations: Insertion or deletion that shifts the reading frame
• Chromosomal mutations: Changes in chromosome structure or number

Mutations can be harmful, beneficial, or neutral. They are the raw material for evolution.

Mutagens include radiation (UV, X-rays), chemicals, and viruses.

title: A Complete Guide to Genetics (Part 5)
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
Transcription and translation are the two steps of protein synthesis.

Transcription (nucleus): DNA → mRNA. RNA polymerase reads DNA and creates a complementary mRNA strand.

Translation (ribosome): mRNA → Protein. Ribosomes read mRNA in groups of three bases (codons). Each codon codes for a specific amino acid. tRNA brings the matching amino acids.

Start codon: AUG (methionine). Stop codons: UAA, UAG, UGA.
subject: Biology

title: A Complete Guide to Genetics (Part 6)
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
Punnett squares predict the probability of offspring inheriting specific traits. A monohybrid cross tracks one trait, a dihybrid cross tracks two.

Dominant alleles (capital letter) mask recessive alleles (lowercase).

Example: T = tall (dominant), t = short (recessive).
Tt × Tt: Genotype ratio 1:2:1 (TT, Tt, tt). Phenotype ratio 3:1 (tall:short).

Incomplete dominance: Neither allele is fully dominant (e.g., red + white = pink).

title: Practice Questions: Genetics (Part 7)
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
Beyond Mendelian genetics:
• Codominance: Both alleles are expressed (e.g., AB blood type)
• Multiple alleles: More than two alleles exist (e.g., blood types A, B, O)
• Polygenic traits: Controlled by multiple genes (e.g., height, skin color)
• Epistasis: One gene masks the expression of another
• Sex-linked traits: Genes on the X chromosome (e.g., color blindness, hemophilia)

title: Mastering Genetics (Part 8)
description: Learn about genetics in Biology.
body:
Genetic engineering involves directly manipulating an organism's DNA. Key tools:

• CRISPR-Cas9: A precise gene-editing tool that can cut DNA at specific locations
• Recombinant DNA: Combining DNA from different organisms (e.g., inserting human insulin gene into bacteria)
• Gene therapy: Replacing faulty genes with healthy copies

Applications include medicine (insulin production, gene therapy), agriculture (GMOs), and research.
subject: Biology

title: Introduction to Ecology
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Ecology studies how organisms interact with each other and their environment. Ecosystems include all living (biotic) and non-living (abiotic) components in an area.

Levels of ecological organization: individual → population → community → ecosystem → biome → biosphere.

Biotic factors: plants, animals, fungi, bacteria.
Abiotic factors: sunlight, temperature, water, soil, air.

title: Quick Quiz: Ecology (Part 2)
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Food chains show the flow of energy through an ecosystem. Producers (plants) convert sunlight into energy through photosynthesis. Consumers eat other organisms. Decomposers break down dead matter.

Food webs are more complex networks of interconnected food chains.

Energy pyramid: Only about 10% of energy passes from one trophic level to the next. The rest is lost as heat. This limits the number of trophic levels to about 4-5.

title: Exercise: Ecology (Part 3)
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Biogeochemical cycles move elements through ecosystems:

Carbon cycle: CO₂ is fixed by plants (photosynthesis), released by respiration, decomposition, and burning fossil fuels.

Nitrogen cycle: N₂ in air → fixed by bacteria → absorbed by plants → consumed by animals → returned by decomposition.

Water cycle: Evaporation → condensation → precipitation → runoff.

Phosphorus cycle: Rocks release phosphorus → absorbed by plants → consumed → returned.

title: Key Concepts: Ecology (Part 4)
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Populations change over time due to birth rate, death rate, immigration, and emigration. Population growth can be exponential (unlimited resources) or logistic (limited resources carrying capacity).

Carrying capacity is the maximum population size an environment can sustain.

Limiting factors: food, water, space, disease, competition, predators.

Density-dependent factors: affected by population density (disease, competition).
Density-independent factors: not affected by density (natural disasters, weather).
subject: Biology

title: A Complete Guide to Ecology (Part 5)
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Species interactions:
• Competition: Both species are harmed (-/-)
• Predation: One benefits, one is harmed (+/-)
• Mutualism: Both benefit (+/+)
• Commensalism: One benefits, one is unaffected (+/0)
• Parasitism: One benefits, one is harmed (+/-)
• Amensalism: One is harmed, one is unaffected (-/0)

Keystone species have a disproportionately large effect on their ecosystem relative to their abundance.

title: Introduction to Ecology (Part 6)
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Biodiversity is the variety of life on Earth. It has three components: genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity.

Biodiversity is important because it:
• Provides ecosystem services (pollination, water purification)
• Supports food security
• Provides medicines and resources
• Helps ecosystems recover from disturbances

Threats to biodiversity: habitat loss, climate change, pollution, invasive species, overexploitation.

title: Practice Questions: Ecology (Part 7)
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Biomes are large-scale ecosystems classified by climate and dominant vegetation:

• Tropical Rainforest: Warm, wet, high biodiversity
• Savanna: Warm, seasonal rainfall, grasses and scattered trees
• Desert: Very dry, extreme temperatures
• Temperate Forest: Moderate climate, deciduous trees
• Taiga (Boreal Forest): Cold, coniferous trees
• Tundra: Very cold, permafrost, no trees
• Grassland: Moderate rainfall, grasses dominant
• Aquatic: Freshwater and marine ecosystems

title: Mastering Ecology (Part 8)
description: Learn about ecology in Biology.
body:
Ecological succession is the process of ecosystem change over time. Primary succession starts on bare rock (after volcanic eruption). Secondary succession starts on existing soil (after fire or farming).

Pioneer species (lichens, mosses) are the first to colonize. They create conditions for other species to move in. The final, stable community is called the climax community.

Example of secondary succession: abandoned farm field → grasses → shrubs → young trees → mature forest.
subject: Biology

title: Understanding The Renaissance
description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
The Renaissance (1400-1600 CE) was a period of cultural, artistic, and intellectual rebirth in Europe after the Middle Ages. It began in Italy and spread across the continent.

What sparked the Renaissance?
• The fall of Constantinople (1453) sent Greek scholars to Italy with ancient texts
• Increased trade with Asia brought wealth to Italian city-states
• The printing press (Gutenberg, ~1440) made books available to more people
• A shift from religious focus to humanism — the study of human potential
subject: World History

title: Practice Questions: The Renaissance (Part 2)
description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
Humanism was the intellectual movement at the heart of the Renaissance. Humanists studied classical Greek and Roman texts on history, philosophy, and literature — works that had been largely ignored during the Middle Ages.

Key humanist ideas:
• Focus on human achievement and potential (not just religious devotion)
• Education in the humanities: grammar, rhetoric, history, poetry, ethics
• Civic engagement: educated citizens should participate in public life

Petrarch (1304-1374) is called the Father of Humanism. He rediscovered Cicero's letters and promoted classical learning.
subject: World History

title: Step by Step: The Renaissance (Part 3)
description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
Renaissance art transformed from flat, religious icons to realistic, three-dimensional scenes. Key innovations:

Linear perspective: Created depth by making parallel lines converge at a vanishing point.
Chiaroscuro: Use of light and shadow to create volume.
Sfumato: Subtle blending of colors and tones.

Master artists:
• Leonardo da Vinci: Mona Lisa, The Last Supper
• Michelangelo: David, Sistine Chapel ceiling
• Raphael: School of Athens
• Botticelli: Birth of Venus
subject: World History

title: At a Glance: The Renaissance (Part 4)
description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
The printing press, invented by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440, was one of the most important inventions in history. It used movable type to print books quickly and cheaply.

Before the printing press: books were hand-copied by monks, rare, and expensive.
After: Books became widely available. By 1500, over 20 million books had been printed.

Impact: Literacy rates rose, knowledge spread faster, the Bible was translated into vernacular languages, and new ideas (like those of the Reformation) spread rapidly.

title: A Complete Guide to The Renaissance (Part 5)
description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
The Renaissance spread from Italy to Northern Europe through trade, travel, and the printing press. Northern Renaissance artists focused more on domestic life and landscapes (while Italians focused on religious and classical themes).

Key Northern figures:
• Albrecht Dürer (Germany): Master of printmaking and engraving
• Jan van Eyck (Flanders): Pioneered oil painting techniques
• Desiderius Erasmus (Netherlands): Christian humanist scholar
• Thomas More (England): Wrote Utopia
• William Shakespeare (England): Playwright, wrote Hamlet, Romeo and Juliet

title: Mastering The Renaissance (Part 6)
description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
The Scientific Revolution began during the Renaissance as thinkers challenged ancient authorities and emphasized observation and experimentation.

Nicolaus Copernicus proposed that the Sun (not Earth) is the center of the solar system — a radical idea that challenged Church teaching.

Galileo Galilei improved the telescope and confirmed Copernicus's model. He was tried by the Church and forced to recant.

Andreas Vesalius revolutionized anatomy by dissecting human cadavers.

title: Practice Questions: The Renaissance (Part 7)
description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
The Medici family of Florence were the most famous patrons of the Renaissance. They were wealthy bankers who funded artists, architects, and scholars.

Cosimo de' Medici (1389-1464) established the family's political power and patronized artists like Donatello and Fra Angelico.

Lorenzo the Magnificent (1449-1492) ruled Florence and supported Botticelli, Leonardo da Vinci, and Michelangelo.

Without wealthy patrons like the Medici, many Renaissance masterpieces would not exist.

description: Learn about the renaissance in World History.
body:
The legacy of the Renaissance:
• Shifted worldview from religious authority to human reason and observation
• Revolutionized art with perspective, realism, and individual expression
• Advanced science through observation and experimentation
• Spread literacy through the printing press
• Laid the foundation for the Enlightenment, Reformation, and modern science
• Vernacular languages gained prominence over Latin in literature and official documents

title: Exploring Ancient Civilizations
description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
Ancient Mesopotamia, between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers (modern Iraq), is called the Cradle of Civilization. Here, the first cities emerged around 3500 BCE.

Key achievements:
• Writing (cuneiform) — the first writing system
• The wheel — revolutionized transport
• Laws — Code of Hammurabi, one of the first written law codes
• Mathematics — base-60 number system (we still use 60 seconds, 60 minutes)
• Irrigation — controlled flooding for farming

title: Test Your Knowledge: Ancient Civilizations (Part 2)
description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
Ancient Egypt developed along the Nile River. The annual flooding of the Nile deposited rich soil (silt) that made farming highly productive.

Egyptian civilization lasted over 3000 years. Key features:
• Pharaohs: Rulers considered living gods
• Pyramids: Massive tombs for pharaohs, built by skilled workers
• Hieroglyphics: Picture-based writing
• Mummification: Preserving bodies for the afterlife
• Papyrus: Early paper made from reeds
• 365-day calendar: Based on the Nile's flood cycle
subject: World History

description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
Ancient Greece is the foundation of Western civilization. The Greeks developed democracy, philosophy, theater, and the Olympic Games.

City-states: Each polis (city-state) was independent. Athens developed democracy, Sparta emphasized military strength.

Athenian democracy: Citizens voted directly on laws. Only free adult men were citizens.

Persian Wars: Greece united to defeat the Persian Empire. The Battle of Marathon (490 BCE) and Thermopylae (480 BCE) are famous.

title: At a Glance: Ancient Civilizations (Part 4)
description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
Greek philosophy explored questions about existence, knowledge, and ethics:

• Socrates (470-399 BCE): Questioned everything through dialogue. 'The unexamined life is not worth living.' Executed for corrupting youth.
• Plato (428-348 BCE): Student of Socrates. Wrote The Republic about ideal government. Founded the Academy.
• Aristotle (384-322 BCE): Student of Plato. Tutored Alexander the Great. Catalogued knowledge from biology to politics. 'All men by nature desire to know.'

title: A Complete Guide to Ancient Civilizations (Part 5)
description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
The Roman Republic (509-27 BCE) was governed by elected officials and had a system of checks and balances. The Senate was a council of wealthy patricians. Consuls (two) held executive power for one year.

The Twelve Tables (450 BCE) were Rome's first written laws, displayed publicly so all citizens knew their rights.

Punic Wars (264-146 BCE): Rome defeated Carthage and became the dominant Mediterranean power.

title: Mastering Ancient Civilizations (Part 6)
description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
The Roman Empire (27 BCE - 476 CE) began when Augustus became the first emperor. The Empire stretched from Britain to Egypt.

Key features:
• Pax Romana: 200 years of peace and prosperity
• Roman law: Foundation for many modern legal systems
• Engineering: Aqueducts, roads, concrete, arches, the Colosseum
• Latin: Became the language of law, science, and the Church
• Christianity: Emerged in the Roman province of Judea and spread throughout the empire

title: Quick Quiz: Ancient Civilizations (Part 7)
description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
Ancient China developed along the Yellow River. Dynasties ruled for thousands of years.

Shang Dynasty (1600-1046 BCE): First recorded dynasty. Developed writing, bronze casting, and ancestor worship.

Zhou Dynasty (1046-256 BCE): Introduced the Mandate of Heaven — the ruler's right to rule was granted by heaven and could be revoked.

Qin Dynasty (221-206 BCE): United China under Emperor Qin Shi Huang. Standardized writing, currency, and measurements. Began the Great Wall.

Han Dynasty (206 BCE-220 CE): Golden age of Chinese culture. Silk Road trade. Invented paper.

title: Introduction to Ancient Civilizations (Part 8)
description: Learn about ancient civilizations in World History.
body:
Mesoamerican civilizations developed independently in Central America.

Olmecs (1200-400 BCE): First major civilization. Built colossal stone heads, developed writing and calendars.

Maya (250-900 CE): Advanced mathematics (concept of zero), accurate calendars, pyramids, and writing system.

Aztecs (1325-1521 CE): Built Tenochtitlan (now Mexico City) on an island. Sophisticated agriculture (chinampas, floating gardens). Conquered by the Spanish under Cortés.

Incas (1438-1533 CE): Largest pre-Columbian empire, in the Andes. Built Machu Picchu, used quipu (knotted cords) for record-keeping.
subject: World History

title: Understanding World Wars
description: Learn about world wars in World History.
body:
World War I (1914-1918) was the first global war, involving most European powers. The immediate cause was the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary.

Underlying causes (MAIN):
• Militarism: Arms race and glorification of military power
• Alliances: Triple Entente (France, UK, Russia) vs Triple Alliance (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy)
• Imperialism: Competition for colonies
• Nationalism: Ethnic groups seeking independence

Trench warfare created a brutal stalemate on the Western Front.

description: Learn about world wars in World History.

WWI introduced modern warfare with devastating new weapons:
• Machine guns: Could fire 600 rounds per minute
• Poison gas: Chlorine and mustard gas caused horrific injuries
• Tanks: First used by the British in 1916
• Airplanes: Used for reconnaissance and later bombing
• Submarines (U-boats): German submarines attacked Allied shipping

The Battle of the Somme (1916) had over 1 million casualties. The Battle of Verdun (1916) had ~700,000 casualties.

title: Practice: World Wars (Part 3)
description: Learn about world wars in World History.
body:
The Treaty of Versailles (1919) ended WWI but imposed harsh terms on Germany:
• War guilt clause: Germany accepted full responsibility
• Reparations: Germany paid 132 billion gold marks
• Territory losses: Germany lost 13% of its land
• Military restrictions: Army limited to 100,000, no air force, no submarines
• League of Nations: Created to prevent future wars (but the US didn't join)

The treaty's harsh terms contributed to economic instability and resentment that fueled the rise of the Nazis.

title: Visual Summary: World Wars (Part 4)
description: Learn about world wars in World History.
body:
World War II (1939-1945) was the deadliest war in history, with 70-85 million casualties.

Key causes:
• Rise of fascism: Hitler in Germany, Mussolini in Italy, militarists in Japan
• Expansionism: Germany annexed Austria and Czechoslovakia; Japan invaded China (1937)
• Appeasement: European powers allowed Hitler's aggression to avoid war
• Failure of the League of Nations: Could not stop aggression

title: Mastering World Wars (Part 5)
description: Learn about world wars in World History.
body:
Major WWII events:
• 1939: Germany invaded Poland. UK and France declared war.
• 1940: Germany conquered France. Battle of Britain — German air force failed to defeat Britain.
• 1941: Germany invaded USSR (Operation Barbarossa). Japan attacked Pearl Harbor. US entered the war.
• 1942: Turning points — Battle of Midway (Pacific), Battle of Stalingrad (Eastern Front), El Alamein (North Africa)
• 1944: D-Day (June 6, Normandy invasion). Allies liberated Western Europe.
• 1945: Germany surrendered (May). Atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Japan surrendered (September).

title: Introduction to World Wars (Part 6)
description: Learn about world wars in World History.
body:
The Holocaust was the systematic genocide of six million Jews by Nazi Germany. Other groups targeted: Roma, disabled people, Slavic people, political opponents, LGBTQ+ individuals, and Jehovah's Witnesses.

Key stages:
• 1933-1938: Discrimination and segregation (Nuremberg Laws, Kristallnacht)
• 1939-1941: Ghettos and concentration camps
• 1941-1945: Death camps with industrial-scale murder (Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor)

Liberation: Allied forces liberated camps in 1945, revealing the full horror to the world.
subject: World History

• Radar: Detected enemy aircraft and ships
• Code-breaking: Allies cracked German Enigma codes
• Atomic bomb: Developed by the Manhattan Project
• Jet engines: First operational jet fighters
• Rockets: German V-2 rockets were precursors to space vehicles
• Penicillin: Mass-produced for the first time, saving countless lives

title: Mastering World Wars (Part 8)
description: Learn about world wars in World History.
body:
The aftermath of WWII reshaped the world:
• Cold War (1947-1991): The US and Soviet Union became superpowers in an ideological struggle
• United Nations (1945): Replaced the League of Nations, aimed to maintain peace
• Decolonization: European empires dissolved; India, African nations, and others gained independence
• Marshall Plan: US rebuilt Western Europe with $13 billion in aid
• Nuremberg Trials: Nazi leaders prosecuted for war crimes and crimes against humanity
• New world order: Bipolar power structure with nuclear deterrence

title: Introduction to Grammar
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Every word in English belongs to one of eight categories called parts of speech:

1. Noun: A person, place, thing, or idea (dog, freedom, Paris)
2. Pronoun: Replaces a noun (she, they, it)
3. Verb: Action or state of being (run, is, believe)
4. Adjective: Describes a noun (blue, tall, exciting)
5. Adverb: Describes a verb, adjective, or adverb (quickly, very, quite)
6. Preposition: Shows relationship (in, on, at, with, between)
7. Conjunction: Connects elements (and, but, or, because)
8. Interjection: Expresses emotion (wow!, oops!, hey!)
subject: English
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Quick Quiz: Grammar (Part 2)
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Sentence structure: A complete sentence needs a subject (who or what) and a predicate (what they do).

Simple sentence: One independent clause. 'The cat sat on the mat.'
Compound sentence: Two independent clauses joined by a conjunction. 'The cat sat on the mat, and the dog watched.'
Complex sentence: One independent clause + one dependent clause. 'When the cat sat on the mat, the dog watched.'

Fragment: An incomplete sentence. 'Sat on the mat.' (no subject)
Run-on: Two independent sentences joined without punctuation.
subject: English
quizOptions: To identify areas that need more practice

flashcards.title: Key Point 1
flashcards.body: Sentence structure: A complete sentence needs a subject (who or what) and a predicate (what they do).

title: Exercise: Grammar (Part 3)
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Verb tenses show when an action happens. English has three main time categories in three aspects:

Simple: 'I walk' (habitual), 'I walked' (past), 'I will walk' (future)
Progressive: 'I am walking' (in progress), 'I was walking' (was in progress), 'I will be walking'
Perfect: 'I have walked' (completed), 'I had walked' (completed before another past action), 'I will have walked'
Perfect Progressive: 'I have been walking' (started and continuing)

Irregular verbs don't follow the -ed pattern: go/went/gone, swim/swam/swum, sing/sang/sung.
subject: English
quizOptions: Check your work against the solution

title: At a Glance: Grammar (Part 4)
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Subject-verb agreement: The subject and verb must match in number. Singular subject → singular verb. Plural subject → plural verb.

Correct: 'The student runs.' 'The students run.'
Incorrect: 'The student run.' 'The students runs.'

Tricky cases:
• Collective nouns: 'The team is playing well.' (treats team as one unit)
• Indefinite pronouns: 'Everyone is here.' (everyone is singular)
• Either/or: 'Either the teacher or the students are wrong.' (verb agrees with the closer subject)
subject: English
quizOptions: The main ideas and how they connect

title: Deep Dive into Grammar (Part 5)
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Punctuation gives structure and clarity to writing:

Period (.): Ends a declarative sentence.
Question mark (?): Ends a direct question.
Exclamation point (!): Shows strong emotion.
Comma (,): Separates items in a list, joins independent clauses with a conjunction, sets off introductory phrases.
Semicolon (;): Joins closely related independent clauses without a conjunction.
Colon (:): Introduces a list, explanation, or quote.
Apostrophe ('): Shows possession (Bob's book) or forms contractions (don't, it's).
Quotation marks (""): Enclose direct speech or quotes.
subject: English

title: A Complete Guide to Grammar (Part 6)
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Pronouns must agree with their antecedent (the word they replace) in number and gender.

Correct: 'Each student should bring his or her book.' or 'All students should bring their books.'
Incorrect: 'Each student should bring their book.' (traditional grammar considers this incorrect, though it's now commonly accepted)

Common pronoun errors:
• Me vs I: 'She gave it to John and me.' (object pronoun, not 'John and I')
• Who vs Whom: 'Who is coming?' (subject) / 'To whom should I give this?' (object)
subject: English

title: Quick Quiz: Grammar (Part 7)
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Modifiers describe or limit other words. Misplaced modifiers create confusion or unintended humor.

Correct: 'The boy who was wearing a red hat walked his dog.'
Misplaced: 'The boy walked his dog wearing a red hat.' (Was the dog wearing a hat?)

Dangling modifiers occur when the modifier doesn't clearly refer to anything in the sentence.
'After reading the book, the movie was disappointing.' (Who read the book?)
Better: 'After reading the book, I found the movie disappointing.'
subject: English
quizOptions: Take a break and come back later

title: Mastering Grammar (Part 8)
description: Learn about grammar in English.
body:
Active vs passive voice:

Active: The subject performs the action. 'The cat chased the mouse.'
Passive: The subject receives the action. 'The mouse was chased by the cat.'

Active voice is usually stronger, clearer, and more direct. Passive voice is useful when:
• The actor is unknown: 'The window was broken.'
• The action is more important than the actor: 'The law was passed in 1964.'
• You want to be diplomatic: 'A mistake was made.' (instead of 'You made a mistake.')

title: A Complete Guide to Writing
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
The five-paragraph essay is the standard structure for academic writing:

1. Introduction: Hook, background, thesis statement
2. Body paragraph 1: First main point with evidence and analysis
3. Body paragraph 2: Second main point with evidence and analysis
4. Body paragraph 3: Third main point with evidence and analysis
5. Conclusion: Restate thesis, summarize points, final thought

The thesis statement is the most important sentence — it states your main argument clearly.

title: Test Your Knowledge: Writing (Part 2)
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
A strong introduction captures attention and sets direction:

Hook strategies:
• Startling statistic: 'Every year, 8 million tons of plastic enter the ocean.'
• Rhetorical question: 'What would you do if you had one day left to live?'
• Anecdote: A short, relevant story
• Quote: From an expert or famous person
• Surprising fact: Something most people don't know

After the hook, provide 2-3 sentences of context, then end with your clear, specific thesis statement.

title: Step by Step: Writing (Part 3)
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
Body paragraphs are the heart of your essay. Each should follow the TEAL structure:

T — Topic sentence: States the paragraph's main point
E — Evidence: Facts, examples, quotes, data
A — Analysis: Explain how the evidence supports your thesis
L — Link: Transition to the next paragraph

Use the ICE method for quotes:
I — Introduce: Who said it and in what context
C — Cite: Provide the quote
E — Explain: Analyze its significance

title: Key Concepts: Writing (Part 4)
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
A strong conclusion leaves a lasting impression:

1. Restate your thesis using different words (don't copy it verbatim)
2. Summarize your three main points (one sentence each)
3. End with a memorable final thought:
   • Broad implication: Why does this topic matter?
   • Call to action: What should the reader do?
   • Future prediction: What might happen next?
   • Full circle: Return to the hook from your introduction

DO NOT introduce new evidence in the conclusion.

title: Exploring Writing (Part 5)
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
Persuasive writing aims to convince the reader to agree with your position. Use:

Ethos (credibility): Establish your authority and trustworthiness. Use reliable sources. Show balanced analysis.

Pathos (emotion): Appeal to the reader's emotions with vivid language, personal stories, and powerful examples.

Logos (logic): Use facts, statistics, reasoning, and clear cause-effect relationships.

Address counterarguments: Acknowledge and refute opposing views to strengthen your argument.

title: Mastering Writing (Part 6)
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
The writing process has five stages:

1. Prewriting: Brainstorm ideas, research, create an outline. Don't worry about organization yet — just get ideas down.

2. Drafting: Write a rough version. Focus on getting your ideas on paper, not perfection. Follow your outline.

3. Revising: Improve content, organization, and clarity. Add details, cut irrelevant parts, reorganize paragraphs. This is the most important stage.

4. Editing: Fix grammar, punctuation, spelling, and sentence structure. Read your work aloud to catch errors.

5. Publishing: Share your final work. Consider formatting, citations, and presentation.
subject: English

title: Test Your Knowledge: Writing (Part 7)
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
Descriptive writing creates vivid mental images using sensory details:

• Sight: Colors, shapes, sizes, movement
• Sound: Volume, pitch, rhythm, silence
• Smell: Fragrant, musty, fresh, acrid
• Touch: Texture, temperature, pressure
• Taste: Sweet, bitter, sour, savory

Show, don't tell:
❌ 'The room was messy.'
✅ 'Clothes covered the chair, papers stacked on every surface, and a half-eaten pizza sat on the desk.'

Use figurative language: similes ('as brave as a lion'), metaphors ('time is a thief'), and personification ('the wind whispered').

title: Introduction to Writing (Part 8)
description: Learn about writing in English.
body:
Writing for different audiences and purposes:

Narrative: Tells a story. First-person or third-person. Plot, characters, setting, conflict, resolution.

Expository: Explains or informs. Objective tone, facts, definitions, examples. No personal opinion.

Persuasive: Convinces the reader. Strong thesis, evidence, counterarguments.

Descriptive: Creates a vivid picture. Sensory details, figurative language.

Adjust your tone (formal vs informal), vocabulary (simple vs technical), and sentence length based on your audience.

title: Exploring Literature
description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
Literary analysis examines how authors use techniques to create meaning. When analyzing a text, consider:

Plot: The sequence of events. Exposition → rising action → climax → falling action → resolution.
Character: Protagonist (main character), antagonist (opposing force), round (complex), flat (simple).
Setting: Time and place, which influences mood and theme.
Theme: The central message or insight about life.
Point of view: First-person (I), third-person limited (follows one character), third-person omniscient (knows all).

title: Check Your Understanding: Literature (Part 2)
description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
Figurative language enriches text beyond literal meaning:

Simile: Comparison using 'like' or 'as'. 'Her eyes sparkled like diamonds.'
Metaphor: Direct comparison without 'like' or 'as'. 'Life is a highway.'
Personification: Giving human qualities to non-human things. 'The sun smiled down on us.'
Hyperbole: Exaggeration for effect. 'I've told you a million times.'
Alliteration: Repeated starting sounds. 'Peter Piper picked...'
Onomatopoeia: Words that sound like what they describe. 'Buzz, click, boom.'
Symbolism: An object representing an abstract idea. A dove = peace.

title: Step by Step: Literature (Part 3)
description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
Poetry analysis: Poetry uses condensed language to convey emotion and meaning.

Meter: The rhythmic pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables. Iambic pentameter (five pairs of unstressed-stressed) is the most common in English poetry.

Rhyme scheme: The pattern of end rhymes (ABAB, AABB, etc.).

Stanza: A group of lines. Couplet (2 lines), quatrain (4), sonnet (14).

Imagery: Language that appeals to the senses.

Tone: The poet's attitude toward the subject.

Famous forms: Sonnet (Shakespearean or Petrarchan), haiku (3 lines, 5-7-5 syllables), free verse (no regular meter or rhyme).

title: Visual Summary: Literature (Part 4)
description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
Shakespeare's works are central to English literature. He wrote 37 plays and 154 sonnets.

Tragedies: Hamlet, Macbeth, Romeo and Juliet, Othello, King Lear. Tragic hero makes a fatal mistake (hamartia) leading to their downfall.

Comedies: A Midsummer Night's Dream, Much Ado About Nothing, Twelfth Night. End in marriage or reconciliation.

Histories: Henry V, Richard III. Based on English historical figures.

Romeo and Juliet: Star-crossed lovers from feuding families. Themes: love, fate, conflict, youth vs age.

title: Introduction to Literature (Part 5)
description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
The novel emerged as a major literary form in the 18th century. Early novels often used epistolary form (letters) and focused on character development and social commentary.

Key early novels:
• Robinson Crusoe (1719) by Daniel Defoe — Adventure, survival
• Pride and Prejudice (1813) by Jane Austen — Social commentary, marriage, class
• Great Expectations (1861) by Charles Dickens — Social criticism, coming of age
• Wuthering Heights (1847) by Emily Brontë — Gothic, passionate, complex narrative

title: Introduction to Literature (Part 6)
description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
Modernist literature (early 1900s) broke from traditional forms in response to a changing world:

Characteristics:
• Stream of consciousness: Writing that mimics the flow of thoughts
• Fragmented narrative: Non-linear timelines
• Focus on inner experience over external plot
• Skepticism toward traditional values

Key modernist works:
• Ulysses by James Joyce
• Mrs Dalloway by Virginia Woolf
• The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald
• The Waste Land by T.S. Eliot

description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
Reading comprehension strategies improve understanding:

Before reading: Preview the text (headings, images, summaries). Set a purpose. Activate prior knowledge.

During reading: Annotate — underline key points, write questions in margins. Summarize paragraphs in your own words. Make predictions and connections.

After reading: Summarize the main ideas. Identify themes. Evaluate the author's purpose and techniques. Discuss with others.

The SQ3R method: Survey, Question, Read, Recite, Review.

title: Mastering Literature (Part 8)
description: Learn about literature in English.
body:
Writing a literary analysis essay:

1. Choose a focus: A specific theme, character, or literary device.
2. Develop a thesis: Your argument about the text (e.g., 'In Macbeth, sleep symbolizes innocence and guilt.')
3. Find evidence: Quotes and specific passages that support your thesis.
4. Analyze, don't summarize: Explain HOW the author's choices create meaning. A summary tells what happens; analysis explains why it matters.
5. Structure: Introduction with thesis → body paragraphs (each analyzing one piece of evidence) → conclusion.

Always integrate quotes smoothly and cite them properly.

title: Exploring Periodic Table
description: Learn about periodic table in Chemistry.
body:
The periodic table organizes all known chemical elements by their atomic number (number of protons). Dmitri Mendeleev developed it in 1869, leaving gaps for undiscovered elements.

Rows are called periods (7 total). Elements in the same period have the same number of electron shells.

Columns are called groups (18 total). Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons and similar chemical properties.

title: Check Your Understanding: Periodic Table (Part 2)
description: Learn about periodic table in Chemistry.
body:
Key groups in the periodic table:

Group 1 — Alkali Metals (Li, Na, K): Highly reactive, 1 valence electron. React violently with water.

Group 2 — Alkaline Earth Metals (Mg, Ca): Reactive, 2 valence electrons.

Group 17 — Halogens (F, Cl, I): Very reactive nonmetals, 7 valence electrons.

Group 18 — Noble Gases (He, Ne, Ar): Unreactive, full outer shell.

Transition Metals (Groups 3-12): Less reactive, form colored compounds. Include iron, copper, gold, silver.

title: Exercise: Periodic Table (Part 3)
description: Learn about periodic table in Chemistry.
body:
Periodic trends help predict element behavior:

• Atomic radius: Increases down a group (more shells), decreases right across a period (more protons pull electrons in).

• Ionization energy: Energy needed to remove an electron. Decreases down a group, increases right across a period.

• Electronegativity: Ability to attract electrons. Increases right and up. Fluorine is the most electronegative element.

• Electron affinity: Energy change when adding an electron. Similar trend to electronegativity.

title: Key Concepts: Periodic Table (Part 4)
description: Learn about periodic table in Chemistry.
body:
Electron configuration describes how electrons are arranged in shells and subshells. The Aufbau principle: electrons fill lowest energy levels first.

Shells: 1, 2, 3, 4... Each holds up to 2n² electrons.

Subshells: s (holds 2), p (holds 6), d (holds 10), f (holds 14).

Electron configuration notation: 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d¹⁰ 4p⁶...

Valence electrons are in the outermost shell and determine bonding behavior.
subject: Chemistry
quizOptions: The main ideas and how they connect

title: Exploring Periodic Table (Part 5)
description: Learn about periodic table in Chemistry.
body:
Metals, nonmetals, and metalloids:

Metals (left side): Shiny, conductive (heat and electricity), malleable, ductile. Most elements are metals.

Nonmetals (right side): Dull, poor conductors, brittle. Include gases (O₂, N₂) and solids (C, S).

Metalloids (staircase line): Have properties of both. Examples: Silicon (Si), Germanium (Ge) — used in semiconductors.

Hydrogen is unique: Placed in Group 1 but behaves like a nonmetal. It's the most abundant element in the universe.

title: Exploring Periodic Table (Part 6)
description: Learn about periodic table in Chemistry.
body:
Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons. They have the same atomic number (protons) but different mass numbers.

Examples:
• Carbon-12 (6 protons + 6 neutrons) — stable
• Carbon-13 (6 + 7) — stable
• Carbon-14 (6 + 8) — radioactive, used for carbon dating

Radioactive isotopes decay at a fixed rate (half-life). Applications: medical imaging, cancer treatment, radiometric dating.

title: Practice Questions: Periodic Table (Part 7)
description: Learn about periodic table in Chemistry.
body:
The concept of moles connects atomic-scale particles to measurable quantities. One mole = 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number).

Molar mass: The mass of one mole of a substance in grams. Numerically equal to the atomic mass.

Example: Carbon's atomic mass is 12.01 amu. Molar mass = 12.01 g/mol. One mole of carbon contains 6.022 × 10²³ atoms and has a mass of 12.01 grams.

Moles = mass (g) / molar mass (g/mol).

title: Deep Dive into Chemical Reactions
description: Learn about chemical reactions in Chemistry.
body:
Chemical reactions involve rearranging atoms to form new substances. Reactants → Products.

Evidence of a reaction:
• Temperature change (exothermic = heat released, endothermic = heat absorbed)
• Color change
• Gas production (bubbles)
• Precipitate formation (solid from solutions)
• Light emission

The law of conservation of mass: Mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. The total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products.

title: Quick Quiz: Chemical Reactions (Part 2)
description: Learn about chemical reactions in Chemistry.
body:
Balancing chemical equations ensures the same number of each atom on both sides. Use coefficients (numbers placed before formulas) to balance.

Steps:
1. Write the unbalanced equation
2. Count atoms on each side
3. Add coefficients to balance one element at a time
4. Balance hydrogen and oxygen last
5. Check your work

Example: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O
Balanced: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O

Tip: Save pure elements for last. They can be balanced with any coefficient.
subject: Chemistry
quizOptions: To identify areas that need more practice

flashcards.body: Balancing chemical equations ensures the same number of each atom on both sides. Use coefficients (numbers placed before formulas) to balance.

title: Hands-On: Chemical Reactions (Part 3)
description: Learn about chemical reactions in Chemistry.
body:
Types of chemical reactions:

1. Synthesis (A + B → AB): Two substances combine. 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl

2. Decomposition (AB → A + B): A compound breaks down. 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂

3. Single displacement (A + BC → AC + B): One element replaces another. Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂

4. Double displacement (AB + CD → AD + CB): Ions swap partners. AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl + NaNO₃

5. Combustion: Hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O

title: At a Glance: Chemical Reactions (Part 4)
description: Learn about chemical reactions in Chemistry.
body:
Stoichiometry calculates the quantities of reactants and products in chemical reactions using mole ratios.

Steps:
1. Write and balance the chemical equation
2. Convert given quantity to moles
3. Use the mole ratio from coefficients to find moles of desired substance
4. Convert moles to the required unit (mass, volume, particles)

Example: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. How many grams of water from 4g of H₂?
4g H₂ = 2 moles H₂. 2:2 ratio = 2 moles H₂O. 2 × 18 = 36g H₂O.

title: Introduction to Chemical Reactions (Part 5)
description: Learn about chemical reactions in Chemistry.
body:
The rate of a chemical reaction depends on several factors:

• Concentration: Higher concentration = more collisions = faster reaction
• Temperature: Higher temperature = more energetic collisions = faster reaction (roughly doubles every 10°C)
• Surface area: Smaller particles = more surface area = faster reaction
• Catalysts: Substances that speed up reactions without being consumed
• Pressure: Higher pressure (for gases) = more collisions = faster reaction

Collision theory: Reactions occur when particles collide with sufficient energy and proper orientation.
subject: Chemistry
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Mastering Chemical Reactions (Part 6)
description: Learn about chemical reactions in Chemistry.
body:
Acids and bases are opposites in chemistry:

Acids: Taste sour, pH < 7, turn blue litmus red. Examples: HCl (hydrochloric), H₂SO₄ (sulfuric), CH₃COOH (acetic).

Bases: Taste bitter, feel slippery, pH > 7, turn red litmus blue. Examples: NaOH (sodium hydroxide), NH₃ (ammonia).

pH scale: 0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly basic). 7 is neutral (pure water).

Neutralization: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.
subject: Chemistry

title: Check Your Understanding: Chemical Reactions (Part 7)
description: Learn about chemical reactions in Chemistry.
body:
Redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions involve electron transfer:

Oxidation: Loss of electrons (OIL — Oxidation Is Loss)
Reduction: Gain of electrons (RIG — Reduction Is Gain)

Oxidizing agent: Accepts electrons (gets reduced)
Reducing agent: Donates electrons (gets oxidized)

Example: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Mg loses electrons (oxidized), O gains electrons (reduced).

Real-world applications: Batteries, rusting, photosynthesis, cellular respiration.

title: Deep Dive into Atomic Structure
description: Learn about atomic structure in Chemistry.
body:
Atoms are the basic units of matter. They consist of a nucleus (protons + neutrons) surrounded by electrons.

Protons: Positive charge, mass ~1 amu, found in nucleus. Number = atomic number = element identity.

Neutrons: No charge, mass ~1 amu, found in nucleus. Number can vary (isotopes).

Electrons: Negative charge, negligible mass, found in orbitals around nucleus. Determine chemical properties.

Most of an atom is empty space. If the nucleus were the size of a marble, the atom would be as wide as a football stadium.
subject: Chemistry
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Quick Quiz: Atomic Structure (Part 2)
description: Learn about atomic structure in Chemistry.
body:
The history of the atomic model shows how science builds on prior knowledge:

Democritus (400 BCE): First proposed that matter is made of indivisible particles called 'atomos.'

Dalton (1803): Solid sphere model. Atoms are solid, indivisible spheres. Each element has unique atoms.

Thomson (1897): Plum pudding model. Discovered electrons. Atoms are positive spheres with negative electrons embedded.

Rutherford (1911): Nuclear model. Gold foil experiment showed atoms have a small, dense nucleus.

Bohr (1913): Planetary model. Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels.

Quantum mechanical (1920s+): Electron cloud model. Electrons exist in probabilistic orbitals, not fixed orbits.
subject: Chemistry
quizOptions: To identify areas that need more practice

title: Step by Step: Atomic Structure (Part 3)
description: Learn about atomic structure in Chemistry.
body:
Quantum numbers describe the location and energy of electrons:

Principal quantum number (n): Main energy level. n = 1, 2, 3... Higher n = higher energy, larger orbital.

Angular momentum quantum number (l): Shape of orbital. l = 0 (s), 1 (p), 2 (d), 3 (f).

Magnetic quantum number (m_l): Orientation in space. Ranges from -l to +l.

Spin quantum number (m_s): Electron spin. +½ or -½. Each orbital holds two electrons with opposite spins.

The Pauli exclusion principle: No two electrons can have the same set of four quantum numbers.
subject: Chemistry

title: Overview: Atomic Structure (Part 4)
description: Learn about atomic structure in Chemistry.
body:
Chemical bonds form when atoms share or transfer electrons:

Ionic bonds: Transfer of electrons. Metal gives electrons to nonmetal. Forms positive and negative ions that attract. Example: NaCl (sodium chloride). High melting point, conducts electricity when dissolved.

Covalent bonds: Sharing of electrons. Between nonmetals. Single (1 pair), double (2 pairs), or triple (3 pairs) bonds. Example: H₂O (water). Lower melting point than ionic compounds.

Metallic bonds: Sea of delocalized electrons. Between metals. Malleable, ductile, conductive. Example: Cu (copper).
subject: Chemistry

title: Mastering Atomic Structure (Part 5)
description: Learn about atomic structure in Chemistry.
body:
Molecular geometry determines how molecules look and behave:

VSEPR theory: Electron pairs repel each other, arranging themselves as far apart as possible around the central atom.

Common shapes:
• Linear: 2 electron groups, 180° (CO₂)
• Trigonal planar: 3 groups, 120° (BF₃)
• Tetrahedral: 4 groups, 109.5° (CH₄)
• Bent: 4 groups with 2 lone pairs (H₂O)
• Trigonal pyramidal: 4 groups with 1 lone pair (NH₃)

Molecular shape affects polarity, which affects boiling point and solubility.
subject: Chemistry
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Mastering Atomic Structure (Part 6)
description: Learn about atomic structure in Chemistry.
body:
Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractions BETWEEN molecules (not inside them):

London dispersion forces: Present in all molecules. Temporary dipoles. Strength increases with molecular size.

Dipole-dipole forces: Between polar molecules. The positive end of one molecule attracts the negative end of another.

Hydrogen bonding: Strong dipole-dipole force when H is bonded to N, O, or F. Responsible for water's unique properties.

Strength order: Hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion.

Stronger IMFs = higher boiling/melting points.
subject: Chemistry

description: Learn about atomic structure in Chemistry.
body:
States of matter and phase changes:

Solid: Fixed shape and volume. Particles vibrate in fixed positions. Crystalline (organized) or amorphous (random).

Liquid: Fixed volume, takes shape of container. Particles slide past each other.

Gas: No fixed shape or volume. Particles move freely and rapidly.

Plasma: Ionized gas, free electrons and ions. Most common state of matter in the universe (stars).

Phase changes: Melting (solid → liquid), freezing (liquid → solid), vaporization (liquid → gas), condensation (gas → liquid), sublimation (solid → gas), deposition (gas → solid).

title: Deep Dive into Triangles
description: Learn about triangles in Geometry.
body:
A triangle is a three-sided polygon. It is the simplest closed shape in geometry. Understanding triangles unlocks trigonometry, engineering, and physics.

Classified by sides:
• Equilateral: All 3 sides equal, all 3 angles 60°
• Isosceles: 2 sides equal, base angles are equal
• Scalene: No sides equal, no angles equal

Classified by angles:
• Acute: All angles < 90°
• Right: One angle = 90°
• Obtuse: One angle > 90°
subject: Geometry
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Check Your Understanding: Triangles (Part 2)
description: Learn about triangles in Geometry.
body:
Triangle Sum Theorem: The three interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180°.

Proof: Draw a line parallel to one side through the opposite vertex. Alternate interior angles show the three angles form a straight line (180°).

Exterior Angle Theorem: The exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles.

Example: If two interior angles are 50° and 70°, the third is 180 - 120 = 60°. The exterior angle next to the 60° is 180 - 60 = 120°, which equals 50° + 70°.
subject: Geometry
quizOptions: To identify areas that need more practice

title: Hands-On: Triangles (Part 3)
description: Learn about triangles in Geometry.
body:
Pythagorean Theorem (right triangles only): a² + b² = c², where c is the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle).

Example: A right triangle has legs of 3 and 4. What is the hypotenuse?
3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25. √25 = 5. The hypotenuse is 5.

Common Pythagorean triples: (3,4,5), (5,12,13), (7,24,25), (8,15,17).

Applications: Finding distances, construction, navigation, and surveying.
subject: Geometry

title: At a Glance: Triangles (Part 4)
description: Learn about triangles in Geometry.
body:
Triangle Inequality Theorem: The sum of any two sides must be greater than the third side for a triangle to exist.

Example: Can sides of 2, 3, and 6 form a triangle? 2 + 3 = 5, which is NOT > 6. No triangle.

Can sides of 4, 6, and 8 form a triangle? 4 + 6 = 10 > 8 ✓, 4 + 8 = 12 > 6 ✓, 6 + 8 = 14 > 4 ✓. Yes.

The side opposite the largest angle is always the longest side.

title: Introduction to Triangles (Part 5)
description: Learn about triangles in Geometry.
body:
Congruent triangles are identical in shape and size. Proving congruence:

SSS (Side-Side-Side): All three sides are equal.
SAS (Side-Angle-Side): Two sides and the included angle are equal.
ASA (Angle-Side-Angle): Two angles and the included side are equal.
AAS (Angle-Angle-Side): Two angles and a non-included side are equal.
HL (Hypotenuse-Leg): For right triangles only.

Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are equal (CPCTC).

title: Deep Dive into Triangles (Part 6)
description: Learn about triangles in Geometry.
body:
Similar triangles have the same shape but different sizes. Corresponding angles are equal, and corresponding sides are proportional.

The ratio of corresponding sides is called the scale factor.

AA (Angle-Angle) Similarity: If two angles are equal, the triangles are similar.
SSS Similarity: If all three side ratios are equal, the triangles are similar.
SAS Similarity: If two sides are proportional and the included angle is equal.

Applications: Indirect measurement (finding heights of tall objects using shadows).

description: Learn about triangles in Geometry.

Special right triangles have fixed side ratios:

45-45-90 triangle: Legs are equal. Hypotenuse = leg × √2.
If leg = 1, hypotenuse = √2. If leg = 5, hypotenuse = 5√2.

30-60-90 triangle: Short leg (opposite 30°) = x, long leg (opposite 60°) = x√3, hypotenuse = 2x.
If short leg = 3, long leg = 3√3, hypotenuse = 6.

These ratios appear frequently in geometry, trigonometry, and real-world applications.

title: Mastering Circles
description: Learn about circles in Geometry.
body:
A circle is the set of all points at a fixed distance (radius) from a center point. Key terms:

• Radius (r): Distance from center to any point on the circle
• Diameter (d): Distance across the circle through center. d = 2r
• Circumference (C): Distance around the circle. C = 2πr = πd
• Area (A): Space inside the circle. A = πr²
• Chord: A line segment connecting two points on the circle
• Arc: Part of the circumference
• Sector: A 'pizza slice' region
• Tangent: A line that touches the circle at exactly one point
subject: Geometry
quizOptions: Understanding the core concept helps solve related problems

title: Quick Quiz: Circles (Part 2)
description: Learn about circles in Geometry.
body:
Central angles and arcs:

A central angle has its vertex at the center of the circle. Its measure equals the measure of its intercepted arc.

Arc measure is in degrees (or radians). A full circle = 360°.

Minor arc: Less than 180°.
Major arc: Greater than 180°.
Semicircle: Exactly 180°.

Arc length = (central angle / 360°) × 2πr.

Example: A 60° central angle in a circle of radius 6. Arc length = (60/360) × 2π(6) = (1/6) × 12π = 2π.

title: Step by Step: Circles (Part 3)
description: Learn about circles in Geometry.
body:
Inscribed angles have their vertex on the circle and sides that are chords.

Inscribed Angle Theorem: An inscribed angle measures half of its intercepted arc.

If an inscribed angle intercepts a semicircle (arc of 180°), the angle is 90°.

Example: An inscribed angle of 30° intercepts an arc of 60°.

Corollary: Inscribed angles that intercept the same arc are equal.
subject: Geometry

title: Visual Summary: Circles (Part 4)
description: Learn about circles in Geometry.
body:
Circle theorems about chords and tangents:

• A radius perpendicular to a chord bisects the chord.
• Equal chords are equidistant from the center.
• The tangent at any point is perpendicular to the radius at that point.
• Two tangents from the same external point are equal in length.

Tangent-secant theorem: The square of the tangent length equals the product of the secant's external part and the whole secant.

title: Introduction to Circles (Part 5)
description: Learn about circles in Geometry.
body:
The equation of a circle in coordinate geometry:

(x - h)² + (y - k)² = r²

Where (h, k) is the center and r is the radius.

Example: Circle centered at (3, -2) with radius 5:
(x - 3)² + (y + 2)² = 25

To find if a point lies on the circle, plug coordinates into the equation.
To find the intersection of a line and circle, substitute the line equation into the circle equation and solve.

title: A Complete Guide to Circles (Part 6)
description: Learn about circles in Geometry.
body:
Area of a sector = (central angle / 360°) × πr².

Area of a segment = Area of sector - Area of triangle.

Example: A 90° sector in a circle of radius 4. Area = (90/360) × π(16) = (1/4) × 16π = 4π.

Applications: calculating pizza slice areas, designing wheels and gears, analyzing planetary orbits.

Radians provide an alternative way to measure angles based on the radius:

π radians = 180°
1 radian ≈ 57.3°

Arc length in radians: s = rθ (where θ is in radians).
Area of sector in radians: A = (1/2)r²θ.

Using radians simplifies calculus and physics equations involving rotation.

Angular velocity: ω = θ/t (radians per second).
Linear velocity: v = rω.
